news_summary: Today's positive news highlights the power of collaboration in driving collective success and reveals fascinating insights into Stonehenge's ancient history through the remarkable journey of its most mysterious stone.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles d'aujourd'hui mettent en lumière le pouvoir de la collaboration pour favoriser la réussite collective et nous livrent des informations fascinantes sur l'histoire ancienne de Stonehenge à travers le parcours remarquable de sa pierre la plus mystérieuse.
news_summary_es: Las buenas noticias de hoy ponen de relieve el poder de la colaboración para impulsar el éxito colectivo y revelan datos fascinantes sobre la historia antigua de Stonehenge a través del extraordinario recorrido de su piedra más misteriosa.

articles.title: Don’t compete, collaborate: why collective funding applications are the future
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 09 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01815-yScientists with disparate expertise writing grants together can identify knowledge gaps and drive progress — but systems must change to incentivize them.
articles.content:
Around the world, funding bodies are reporting huge increases in the number of researchers applying for grants. A growing scientific workforce, pressure on researchers to secure funding and surging use of artificial-intelligence models that are slashing the time needed to write credible proposals are heaping pressure on a system that can barely cope.

As application numbers rise, success rates fall. The result is a hypercompetitive funding system. Scientists waste valuable time writing grants that are unlikely to be successful. Some pursue fashionable topics to increase their chances of success, sidelining other essential research, such as replication studies.

Could agentic AI topple grant-funding systems?

Systemic change is needed. In my view, as someone who explores the benefits of coordination in science, researchers need to begin to work collectively, rather than competitively, for funding — including with their rivals.

Last year, my PhD supervisor and I set out to help 12 metascientists to collaboratively apply for funding. Collaboration typically involves colleagues from within a scientist’s networks, but our group included competitors with disparate expertise who would not usually apply for funding together.

We asked the participants to propose and evaluate potential grant topics, deciding together which of the projects would best improve science in their field. This research-prioritization process is already used to steer the direction of research by some major funders, such as the US National Institutes of Health, and in some fields that depend on large-scale shared infrastructure, such as accelerators in particle physics. It enables participants to identify challenges that they collectively deem important, including those that would be too complex or burdensome to pursue individually.

We then adapted the proposals to ensure that they included research interests shared by groups of collaborators. Collectively, the group submitted two grant proposals, both of which won funding.

How to secure philanthropic funding in a competitive climate

I think that such collective funding applications can accelerate scientific progress. Papers with a large number of authors are often more highly cited — perhaps, one study suggests, because working in large groups improves quality (M. Thelwall et al. J. Assoc. Inf. Sci. Technol. 74, 791–810; 2023). Collective research prioritization can help to unify fragmented research lines and identify fresh ways to respond to societal challenges. It also breeds consistency in terms of which measures are used and reported, allowing individual studies to be more easily combined and compared.

Individual participants benefit, too. In our group, all collaborators shared tools and existing data, and discussed ongoing projects, which avoided ‘scooping’ — when a team reports results on the same topic before a rival — and duplication of work. Participants told me that collaboration on a project that had been deemed collectively important made their work feel meaningful, increasing job satisfaction.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful shift in scientific research funding from hypercompetitive individual grant applications to collaborative, collective funding proposals. This systemic change has broad positive implications for accelerating scientific progress, reducing wasted effort, and improving job satisfaction among researchers. The story provides detailed context and examples of successful collective funding applications, highlighting a significant and inspiring innovation in research culture with potential large-scale benefits for society.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find success by working together on grant applications
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around the world, many more scientists are applying for research funding than before. This has made the system very competitive, with fewer grants getting approved. Scientists spend a lot of time writing proposals that often don’t get funded, and some important research is overlooked.

**What happened** – A group of 12 scientists with different skills decided to work as a team to apply for funding together, even though they usually compete with each other. They chose research topics that were important to all of them and combined their ideas into two joint grant proposals. Both proposals were successful and received funding.

**Impact** – This way of applying for grants shows that working together can help scientists focus on bigger problems that are hard to solve alone. It also saves time by sharing tools and data, and avoids repeating the same work. The scientists said working on shared projects made their work feel more meaningful and enjoyable.

**What's next step** – If more scientists and funding organizations support collective grant applications, it could change how research is done. This approach might lead to faster scientific discoveries and better use of resources by encouraging teamwork instead of competition.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists collaborating on grant proposals can improve research progress and make better use of time and resources than competing alone.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques réussissent en collaborant sur des demandes de financement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Partout dans le monde, de plus en plus de scientifiques demandent des financements pour leurs recherches. Cela rend le système très compétitif, avec moins de subventions accordées. Les scientifiques passent beaucoup de temps à écrire des propositions qui sont souvent refusées, et certaines recherches importantes sont négligées.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un groupe de 12 scientifiques aux compétences différentes a décidé de travailler en équipe pour demander un financement ensemble, alors qu’ils sont normalement en compétition. Ils ont choisi des sujets de recherche importants pour tous et ont regroupé leurs idées en deux propositions communes. Les deux ont été acceptées et financées.

**Impact** – Cette façon de demander des financements montre que travailler ensemble aide à se concentrer sur de grands problèmes difficiles à résoudre seuls. Cela fait aussi gagner du temps en partageant outils et données, et évite de refaire le même travail. Les scientifiques ont dit que travailler sur des projets communs rendait leur travail plus important et agréable.

**Prochaine étape** – Si plus de scientifiques et d’organismes de financement soutiennent les demandes collectives, cela pourrait changer la manière de faire la recherche. Cette méthode pourrait accélérer les découvertes scientifiques et mieux utiliser les ressources en favorisant la coopération plutôt que la compétition.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Collaborer sur les demandes de financement permet aux scientifiques de mieux avancer dans leurs recherches et d’utiliser plus efficacement leur temps et leurs moyens que s’ils étaient en concurrence.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos logran éxito al colaborar en solicitudes de financiamiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En todo el mundo, cada vez más científicos solicitan fondos para sus investigaciones. Esto ha hecho que el sistema sea muy competitivo, con menos becas aprobadas. Los científicos dedican mucho tiempo a escribir propuestas que a menudo no son financiadas, y algunas investigaciones importantes quedan fuera.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de 12 científicos con diferentes habilidades decidió trabajar en equipo para solicitar fondos juntos, aunque normalmente compiten entre sí. Eligieron temas de investigación importantes para todos y unieron sus ideas en dos propuestas conjuntas. Ambas fueron aprobadas y financiadas.

**Impacto** – Esta forma de solicitar fondos muestra que trabajar juntos ayuda a enfocarse en problemas grandes que son difíciles de resolver solos. También ahorra tiempo al compartir herramientas y datos, y evita repetir trabajo. Los científicos dijeron que trabajar en proyectos comunes hizo que su trabajo se sintiera más valioso y satisfactorio.

**Próximo paso** – Si más científicos y organizaciones financiadoras apoyan las solicitudes colectivas, esto podría cambiar cómo se hace la investigación. Este enfoque podría acelerar los descubrimientos científicos y usar mejor los recursos fomentando la cooperación en lugar de la competencia.

**Resumen en una frase** – Colaborar en solicitudes de financiamiento permite a los científicos avanzar mejor en sus investigaciones y usar su tiempo y recursos de forma más eficiente que compitiendo solos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of diverse, interconnected hands made of glowing puzzle pieces fitting seamlessly together around a shared, radiant seedling growing from an open book, symbolizing collaborative scientific growth and collective funding nurturing new ideas.

articles.title: Stonehenge's most mysterious stone traveled 700 kilometers across Britain
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered new evidence that Stonehenge’s six-ton Altar Stone was deliberately transported hundreds of kilometers from Scotland by ancient people. The feat would have required extraordinary planning, teamwork, and determination, revealing a surprisingly sophisticated level of organization thousands of years ago.
articles.content:
A new study led by researchers at Curtin University offers fresh insight into one of Stonehenge's most enduring mysteries: how a massive stone made its way across Britain to become part of the famous monument.

The focus of the research is the Altar Stone, a six-ton sandstone megalith located at the center of Stonehenge. Previous work suggested the stone originated in northeast Scotland, roughly 700 kilometers (435 miles) from Salisbury Plain, making its presence at Stonehenge one of the most remarkable examples of long-distance transport in prehistoric Europe.

The latest findings strengthen the idea that ancient people, not natural forces, were responsible for moving the enormous stone across challenging terrain.

Tracing the Altar Stone's Ancient Journey

The study builds on earlier research that ruled out glaciers as the primary means of transporting the stone. To further investigate, scientists combined mineral grain dating techniques with computer models of ancient ice sheets. Their goal was to determine exactly where the stone came from and whether glaciers could have carried it southward during the last Ice Age.

According to the results, glaciers may have moved rocks from Scotland part of the way, possibly reaching Dogger Bank in what is now the North Sea. However, the models show there was no realistic glacial route that could have delivered the Altar Stone directly to southern England.

That leaves a significant portion of the journey unexplained by natural processes alone.

Evidence Points to Human Transport

Co-lead author Dr. Anthony Clarke, from the Timescales of Minerals Systems Group within Curtin's School of Earth and Planetary Sciences, said the evidence indicates the stone's movement was intentional rather than accidental.

"Rather than being carried naturally by ice, the evidence points to a deliberate, carefully planned movement across a challenging and varied landscape," Dr. Clarke said.

"Our modeling shows glaciers may have transported rocks part of the way during the last Ice Age -- potentially as far as Dogger Bank in the North Sea -- but not into southern England, meaning the stone would still have needed to be moved hundreds of kilometers by people.

"The research indicates there were no viable glacial pathways linking the source region directly to Stonehenge, reinforcing the conclusion that human transport was required.

"Instead, this suggests the stone was likely moved in stages, potentially combining overland hauling with river or coastal transport where possible."

A Remarkable Feat of Neolithic Organization

Moving a six-ton stone over such a vast distance would have been an enormous undertaking for Neolithic communities. Researchers believe the journey likely involved multiple stages and required careful coordination among groups living across different regions.

Dr. Clarke said the findings highlight a level of planning and cooperation that may have been greater than previously recognized.

"Transporting a stone of this size over such a long distance would have required planning, coordination and a deep understanding of the landscape -- not to mention tremendous determination," Dr. Clarke said.

"The study demonstrates how combining geological analysis with computer modeling can help resolve long-standing questions about how Stonehenge was built."

Researchers plan to continue investigating the Altar Stone's origins by identifying its precise source location in northeast Scotland and exploring the routes prehistoric people may have used to transport it.

The study involved collaboration between researchers from Curtin University, Sheffield Hallam University, the University of Sheffield, Wessex Archaeology, and the University of Bristol.
articles.reasoning: The article reveals a significant archaeological discovery about Stonehenge, demonstrating remarkable ancient human ingenuity and cooperation. This insight into prehistoric human organization and capability has broad cultural and historical significance, inspiring curiosity and appreciation for human history.
articles.personality_title: Scientists reveal ancient people moved Stonehenge’s giant Altar Stone over 700 km
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Stonehenge is a famous ancient monument in England made of large stones. One of its stones, called the Altar Stone, weighs six tons and was thought to come from far away in Scotland, about 700 kilometers (435 miles) away.

**What happened** – New research by scientists at Curtin University studied the Altar Stone to find out if natural forces like glaciers moved it or if people did. They used special dating methods and computer models to track the stone’s journey. The study showed glaciers could only move the stone part of the way, not all the way to Stonehenge. This means ancient people had to carry or transport the stone across most of the distance.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that people living thousands of years ago had to work together and plan carefully to move such a heavy stone over a long and difficult route. It changes how we understand their skills and organization. Instead of the stone arriving by accident, it was brought on purpose, showing surprising teamwork and knowledge of the land.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to find the exact place in northeast Scotland where the stone came from. They also plan to study how people might have moved it, possibly using rivers or coastal areas to help carry the heavy stone in stages.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Ancient people deliberately moved Stonehenge’s massive Altar Stone over 700 kilometers, showing advanced planning and cooperation long ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques révèlent que l’Ancien Altar Stone de Stonehenge a été déplacé sur 700 km
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Stonehenge est un monument ancien célèbre en Angleterre, composé de grandes pierres. L’une d’elles, appelée l’Altar Stone, pèse six tonnes et viendrait d’Écosse, à environ 700 kilomètres (435 miles) de là.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – De nouvelles recherches menées par l’Université Curtin ont étudié si ce bloc a été déplacé par des forces naturelles comme les glaciers ou par des humains. Ils ont utilisé des méthodes de datation et des modèles informatiques. L’étude montre que les glaciers n’ont pu déplacer la pierre qu’en partie, ce qui signifie que des personnes l’ont transportée sur la majorité du trajet.

**Impact** – Cette découverte révèle que les peuples anciens avaient besoin de beaucoup de coordination et de planification pour déplacer une pierre aussi lourde sur une longue distance. Cela change notre vision de leurs compétences et de leur organisation. La pierre n’est pas arrivée par hasard, mais a été déplacée volontairement, montrant un travail d’équipe et une bonne connaissance du territoire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent identifier précisément son lieu d’origine dans le nord-est de l’Écosse. Ils étudieront aussi comment les anciens ont pu la transporter, peut-être en utilisant des rivières ou la côte pour l’aider par étapes.

**En une phrase** – Des personnes anciennes ont délibérément déplacé la lourde Altar Stone de Stonehenge sur 700 kilomètres, montrant une organisation avancée il y a longtemps.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos revelan que antiguos pueblos trasladaron la enorme Piedra del Altar de Stonehenge 700 km
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Stonehenge es un monumento antiguo famoso en Inglaterra hecho de grandes piedras. Una de ellas, llamada la Piedra del Altar, pesa seis toneladas y se cree que viene de Escocia, a unos 700 kilómetros (435 millas) de distancia.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Curtin estudiaron si la piedra fue movida por fuerzas naturales como glaciares o por personas. Usaron técnicas de datación y modelos por computadora. El estudio mostró que los glaciares solo pudieron mover la piedra parcialmente, por lo que personas antiguas tuvieron que transportarla la mayor parte del camino.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo demuestra que las personas hace miles de años tenían que trabajar en equipo y planear cuidadosamente para mover una piedra tan pesada por una ruta larga y difícil. Cambia cómo entendemos sus habilidades y organización. La piedra no llegó por accidente, sino que fue llevada a propósito, mostrando cooperación y conocimiento del terreno.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren encontrar el lugar exacto en el noreste de Escocia de donde proviene la piedra. También planean estudiar cómo la movieron, posiblemente usando ríos o la costa para ayudar a transportarla en etapas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los antiguos trasladaron deliberadamente la enorme Piedra del Altar de Stonehenge más de 700 kilómetros, demostrando planificación y cooperación avanzada hace mucho tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting showing a massive sandstone megalith resting on a wooden sled, being carefully hauled across a varied prehistoric British landscape that transitions from rugged northeast Scottish hills through winding rivers and gentle plains toward the distant silhouette of Stonehenge’s iconic stone circle under a soft, golden sky.
